grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch dt.davis.problems, from:now() - 30m
| fields event.id, display_id, event.status, event.start, event.end, event.category, event.description, affected_entity_ids, related_entity_ids
| filter if("ACTIVE" == "ALL", TRUE, else:event.status == "ACTIVE")
| sort event.start
| limit 200

tool.name: query-problems
tool.title: Root Cause Agent
tool.description:
Query all Davis Problems.
When querying Davis problems, it's important to understand their lifecycle and how to filter results effectively.
Within a problem lifecycle, each problem has a start time. If the end time is not set, the problem is considered ACTIVE. Once an end time is defined, the problem is considered CLOSED.
Only the 200 most recent problems are returned.

includeTypes.description: If true the DQL query result types will be included in the result. Default value is true.

history.description:
Specifies the time range in minutes (m), hours (h), or days (d) to look back when querying Davis problems:
problems with a timestamp in the range [now-history, now] are returned.
Integer hours to look back from now (e.g., 30m = last half an hour, 5h = last 5 hours, 2d = last 2 days, 7d = last week)
**DO NOT** use values greater than 60 days unless explicitly requested by the user.

status.description:
the status of the problem to query. The value can be one of the following values:
ACTIVE, CLOSED or ALL.
If ALL is provided then both ACTIVE and CLOSED problems are returned.
The default value is ALL

outputSchema.description: Result of executing a DQL query against Dynatrace Grail

records.description: The records returned by the DQL query.

types.description: The types for the records returned by the DQL query.

metadata.description: Metadata about the query execution and results from Grail

outputSchema.title: Grail DQL Query Result

_meta.customerFacingDescription: Explore the 200 most recent problems, active or closed.

tool.name: get-entity-name
tool.title: Smartscape Agent
tool.description: Provides the human readable name for a given entity id.

includeTypes.description: If true the DQL query result types will be included in the result. Default value is true.

entityType.description:
The entity type matching the entity ID. For instance dt.entity.host.
The entity type can be extracted from the entity ID:
  take the part before the first hyphen, make it lower case, and prefix it with "dt.entity.".
  Examples: entity id "HOST-45EC258BE1539C76" => entity type dt.entity.host.
  entity id "SERVICE-1234567890ABCDEF" => entity type dt.entity.service.
  entity id "KUBERNETES_CLUSTER-1234567890ABCDEF" => entity type dt.entity.kubernetes_cluster.
  entity id "PROCESS_GROUP_INSTANCE-F756DBCDB303B1FE" => entity type dt.entity.process_group_instance.

entityId.description: an entity ID. For instance HOST-45EC258BE1539C76

content.text:
{"text":"A **global outage** in the context of an HTTP monitor's **availability** occurs when the monitor is unavailable at all configured locations simultaneously. Below are the key details:

### **Global Outage Details**
1. **Definition**: A global outage is triggered when all locations assigned to the monitor fail to execute successfully.
2. **Problem Generation**:
   - By default, a global outage problem is generated when all locations fail during a single execution.
   - You can configure the number of consecutive failures (1 to 5) required to trigger a global outage problem.
3. **Resolution**:
   - A global outage problem is resolved when there are as many consecutive successful executions as the configured number of failed executions.
   - Even after resolving a global outage, individual locations may still experience failures. Local outage rules can be configured to monitor these.
4. **Availability Calculation**:
   - The **Availability** card in the HTTP monitor details page shows overall availability across all locations, including annotations for global outages.
   - Outage duration is calculated as the sum of all downtimes in the selected timeframe, excluding overlapping downtimes.

### **How to View Global Outage Details**
1. Navigate to the **Availability** section of the HTTP monitor details page.
2. Look for red bars in the timeline, which indicate global outages.
3. Hover over the red bar to see details about the outage, including affected locations and duration.
4. Select the bar to view problem details in the **Problems** app.

### **Configuration**
- To enable or customize global outage detection:
  1. Go to the monitor's settings.
  2. Enable **Generate a problem and send an alert when this monitor is unavailable at all configured locations**.
  3. Specify the number of consecutive failures required to trigger a global outage.

For more information, refer to the **Availability** and **Outage Handling** sections in the HTTP monitor documentation.","messageToken":"4593ac05-c039-470f-9551-eaa984ae3a8c","status":"SUCCESSFUL","state":{"version":"0.3","conversationId":"b06426ba-12fa-4924-8437-e6564812dc4b","skillName":"recommender","history":[{"role":"user","text":"Http monitor global outage AVAILABILITY"},{"role":"ai","text":"A **global outage** in the context of an HTTP monitor's **availability** occurs when the monitor is unavailable at all configured locations simultaneously. Below are the key details:

### **Global Outage Details**
1. **Definition**: A global outage is triggered when all locations assigned to the monitor fail to execute successfully.
2. **Problem Generation**:
   - By default, a global outage problem is generated when all locations fail during a single execution.
   - You can configure the number of consecutive failures (1 to 5) required to trigger a global outage problem.
3. **Resolution**:
   - A global outage problem is resolved when there are as many consecutive successful executions as the configured number of failed executions.
   - Even after resolving a global outage, individual locations may still experience failures. Local outage rules can be configured to monitor these.
4. **Availability Calculation**:
   - The **Availability** card in the HTTP monitor details page shows overall availability across all locations, including annotations for global outages.
   - Outage duration is calculated as the sum of all downtimes in the selected timeframe, excluding overlapping downtimes.

### **How to View Global Outage Details**
1. Navigate to the **Availability** section of the HTTP monitor details page.
2. Look for red bars in the timeline, which indicate global outages.
3. Hover over the red bar to see details about the outage, including affected locations and duration.
4. Select the bar to view problem details in the **Problems** app.

### **Configuration**
- To enable or customize global outage detection:
  1. Go to the monitor's settings.
  2. Enable **Generate a problem and send an alert when this monitor is unavailable at all configured locations**.
  3. Specify the number of consecutive failures required to trigger a global outage.

For more information, refer to the **Availability** and **Outage Handling** sections in the HTTP monitor documentation."}]},"metadata":{"notifications":[],"sources":[{"title":"HTTP monitors reporting results","url":"https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/synthetic-on-grail/synthetic-app/synthetic-details-for-http-monitors","type":"Documentation"},{"title":"HTTP monitors reporting results","url":"https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/synthetic-monitoring/analysis-and-alerting/synthetic-details-for-http-monitors-classic","type":"Documentation"},{"title":"Configure HTTP monitors","url":"https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/synthetic-monitoring/http-monitors-classic/configure-http-monitors-classic","type":"Documentation"},{"title":"Create and configure an HTTP monitor","url":"https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/synthetic-on-grail/synthetic-app/create-and-configure-an-http-monitor","type":"Documentation"}]}}

sources.title: Create and configure an HTTP monitor

structuredContent.text:
A **global outage** in the context of an HTTP monitor's **availability** occurs when the monitor is unavailable at all configured locations simultaneously. Below are the key details:

### **Global Outage Details**
1. **Definition**: A global outage is triggered when all locations assigned to the monitor fail to execute successfully.
2. **Problem Generation**:
   - By default, a global outage problem is generated when all locations fail during a single execution.
   - You can configure the number of consecutive failures (1 to 5) required to trigger a global outage problem.
3. **Resolution**:
   - A global outage problem is resolved when there are as many consecutive successful executions as the configured number of failed executions.
   - Even after resolving a global outage, individual locations may still experience failures. Local outage rules can be configured to monitor these.
4. **Availability Calculation**:
   - The **Availability** card in the HTTP monitor details page shows overall availability across all locations, including annotations for global outages.
   - Outage duration is calculated as the sum of all downtimes in the selected timeframe, excluding overlapping downtimes.

### **How to View Global Outage Details**
1. Navigate to the **Availability** section of the HTTP monitor details page.
2. Look for red bars in the timeline, which indicate global outages.
3. Hover over the red bar to see details about the outage, including affected locations and duration.
4. Select the bar to view problem details in the **Problems** app.

### **Configuration**
- To enable or customize global outage detection:
  1. Go to the monitor's settings.
  2. Enable **Generate a problem and send an alert when this monitor is unavailable at all configured locations**.
  3. Specify the number of consecutive failures required to trigger a global outage.

For more information, refer to the **Availability** and **Outage Handling** sections in the HTTP monitor documentation.

history.text:
A **global outage** in the context of an HTTP monitor's **availability** occurs when the monitor is unavailable at all configured locations simultaneously. Below are the key details:

### **Global Outage Details**
1. **Definition**: A global outage is triggered when all locations assigned to the monitor fail to execute successfully.
2. **Problem Generation**:
   - By default, a global outage problem is generated when all locations fail during a single execution.
   - You can configure the number of consecutive failures (1 to 5) required to trigger a global outage problem.
3. **Resolution**:
   - A global outage problem is resolved when there are as many consecutive successful executions as the configured number of failed executions.
   - Even after resolving a global outage, individual locations may still experience failures. Local outage rules can be configured to monitor these.
4. **Availability Calculation**:
   - The **Availability** card in the HTTP monitor details page shows overall availability across all locations, including annotations for global outages.
   - Outage duration is calculated as the sum of all downtimes in the selected timeframe, excluding overlapping downtimes.

### **How to View Global Outage Details**
1. Navigate to the **Availability** section of the HTTP monitor details page.
2. Look for red bars in the timeline, which indicate global outages.
3. Hover over the red bar to see details about the outage, including affected locations and duration.
4. Select the bar to view problem details in the **Problems** app.

### **Configuration**
- To enable or customize global outage detection:
  1. Go to the monitor's settings.
  2. Enable **Generate a problem and send an alert when this monitor is unavailable at all configured locations**.
  3. Specify the number of consecutive failures required to trigger a global outage.

For more information, refer to the **Availability** and **Outage Handling** sections in the HTTP monitor documentation.

args.briefing_text: Incident P-26063 resolved. Rollback to bd01e4d2cbfddad00b3aa0c7e77d97f0563a3c07 succeeded. 4 requests were affected over 4 minutes. Root cause was 'Switch rollback build from Cloud Build to Docker direct push'. Service is healthy.

args.title: POST-INCIDENT REPORT [P-26063]: Http monitor global outage
args.body:
RCA Verdict: Root Cause: commit `bd01e4d2cbfddad00b3aa0c7e77d97f0563a3c07` — 'Switch rollback build from Cloud Build to Docker direct push'. DQL confirms availability dropped to 0% at `2026-06-08T01:50:00Z`, `41` min after deploy. Anomaly detector confirms deviation from baseline at `2026-06-08T01:45Z`.

Impact: ~4 failed requests · Duration: 4 min · Blast radius: entity only · Peak error rate: 100%

Timeline:
- 2026-06-08T01:08:49Z: Suspect commit `bd01e4d2cbfddad00b3aa0c7e77d97f0563a3c07` deployed.
- 2026-06-08T01:45Z: Anomaly detector first identified deviation from baseline (drop in availability).
- 2026-06-08T01:50:19Z: Dynatrace problem P-26063 detected and VoiceOps initiated.
- 2026-06-08T01:54:28Z: Rollback to previous working commit `bd01e4d2cbfddad00b3aa0c7e77d97f0563a3c07` completed successfully.

Anomaly Findings: Anomaly detector confirmed a deviation from the baseline in HTTP monitor availability starting at `2026-06-08T01:45Z`, approximately 36 minutes after the suspect commit.

Prevention Recommendations:
- More rigorous testing of CI/CD pipeline changes, especially those related to deployment mechanisms.
- Canary deployments for critical services.
- Enhanced pre-deployment checks for synthetic monitors.
